entry.title: Mastering SBPA Approvals : A Deep Dive into Wait Steps, Script Tasks, and API Integrations , Part 2
entry.content:
In our previous blog post, "Mastering Complex Approvals: A Deep Dive into Wait Steps, Script Tasks, and API Integrations," my colleague & SAP Mentor @mohit_bansal3  tackled a common but challenging business requirement: the "all-or-nothing" approval process.

We explored how SAP Build Process Automation, leveraging features like Wait Steps with API Triggering and workflow context updates via API, can handle scenarios where multiple approvals are needed simultaneously, and a single rejection stops the entire workflow.

We walked through a procurement example where both Department Managers and the Finance Department had to unanimously approve a request before it could proceed.

Now, in this second blog, we will demonstrate the step-by-step testing process for the solution we developed. This will allow you to understand the entire development flow more deeply, from concept to validation. Let's dive into the testing for the previous blog's implementation!

Handling Approval Outcomes: Positive and Negative Scenarios

The "All-or-Nothing" Positive Scenario : Lets Start the complete Testing Process

Let's first explore the "positive scenario," where every approver gives their stamp of approval. In this ideal outcome, once all required approvals are secured, the main workflow successfully completes its designated task. This seamless progression is the goal of our "All-or-Nothing" design, ensuring that every necessary check is performed before finalization.

Approval Process Triggering
As you know from our previous blog, for demonstration purposes, we created a simple form within SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA). 

Main process upon submission of request: Context and log  

You'll notice two approvers listed, which will initiate two parallel workflows. We covered the specifics of these parallel workflows in a previous blog post. The determination of these approvers can come from a decision table or your own custom logic.

The key takeaway here is that we've dynamically fetched two approvers, demonstrating the adaptable nature of this step.  it can handle any number of approvers.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  Dynamically Triggering Secondary Workflows

We're now kicking off secondary workflows dynamically, and the number of these workflows depends entirely on the number of approvers.

For our current scenario, since we have two approvers, this process will trigger two parallel secondary workflows.

Each of these workflows will then send an individual approval request directly to each approver's My Inbox.

Dynamically Secondary Workflow Triggered via APIMy Inbox View
Here's a quick look at My Inbox. For this demonstration, we've assigned the same approver.

That's why you see two work items in this approver's inbox. In a real-world scenario, these would be two separate workflow approval items, each in the respective approver's inbox.

Crucially, the main workflow won't complete until both approvals are in.

Workflow Progress After First Approval
The screenshot below shows the workflow log after the first approver has taken action. As you can see, the workflow isn't complete yet; it's waiting for the second secondary workflow to be actioned.

The main workflow will only move forward once both approvals are done.

But before we get ahead of ourselves, let's examine the workflow log immediately after that initial approvals.

Updating the Main Workflow Context after each approval:  SBPA API used as action
We'll be updating the main workflow's context after each approval and then comparing it against the total required count.

Here's a quick glimpse of what happens to the workflow context once the first approval is complete.

You can see the "donecount" increasing, which we'll use to compare against the total number of approvals needed.

We achieve this by updating the main workflow via its API, a process we've already covered in a previous blog post.

The Final Approval and Workflow Completion
Now, let's go a step deeper and understand the flow when the second, and final, approver takes action.

Once they approve, you'll see that the secondary workflow also completes, which then triggers the wait step in the main workflow.

This wait step is triggered via an API, a process we covered in detail in a previous blog post.

Understanding this particular use case of the wait step with an API is a critical aspect of this blog's core message.

Workflow Context After All Approvals
Now, let's carefully examine how the workflow context gets updated once all approvals are complete.

This is the crucial step that triggers the wait step and allows our main workflow to proceed.

In this scenario, the "donecount" becomes equal to the total number of required approvals, which in turn triggers the wait step API.

Handling Approval Outcomes: Positive and Negative Scenarios

so far We've explored the "positive scenario," where all approvers green-light the requests, leading to the main workflow's successful completion.

But what happens when an approver rejects a request?

Imagine a scenario where just one approver rejects. In such a case, the entire workflow must be rejected. My colleague, Mohit Bansal, detailed the design considerations for this rejection scenario in a previous blog post, so from a testing perspective, the approach remains similar.

However, in a rejection scenario, we need to take crucial additional steps:

  • Call the workflow cancel API: This immediately stops the main workflow.
  • Cancel any remaining open instances of secondary workflows: This is critical for designing a robust, production-ready complex workflow. Without canceling these, you could have orphaned workflow instances consuming resources or causing confusion.

  •  


    Here's a quick look at how the system responds when any approver rejects: the main workflow is canceled, and all remaining open secondary workflow instances are also terminated.

We hope you enjoyed our blog series! We dove deep into complex workflow approval scenarios, exploring how to effectively leverage wait steps and workflow APIs to achieve robust "all-or-nothing" approval processes.

Please feel free to leave comments and share your thoughts. A big thank you to my colleague @mohit_bansal3  for his contributions, and stay tuned for more blogs on the latest innovations in this area!

Regards,

Adwait Fadnavis

entry.title: Creating a Sunburst Chart in SAP Analytics Cloud Using R and Plotly
entry.content:
A Sunburst chart is a powerful visualization technique for representing hierarchical data. It starts from a central root node and radiates outward to represent the hierarchy across multiple levels. In SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), you can create this type of chart using R scripting and Plotly, providing a dynamic and interactive experience.

This guide walks you through the steps to build a Sunburst chart inside an SAC story using R.

What Is a Sunburst Chart?

A Sunburst chart visualizes multilevel categorical data in a circular layout. Each level of the hierarchy is represented by one ring or circle, with the innermost circle being the root node. Plotly in R provides excellent support for building dynamic Sunburst visualizations.

Step 1: Enable R Integration in SAC

Before using R scripts in SAC:

  1. Open the Main Menu.
  2. Navigate to System > Administration > External Systems.
  3. Ensure the R connection is enabled.

If not, refer to the official SAP guide for integration:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/8d589100aea341a58a5c0916bbef022b.html

Step 2: Insert an R Visualization in Your Story

  1. Create or open a story.
  2. Go to the Insert section, click the +, then choose R Visualization.
  3. A tile will be added to your story.
  4. Click the tile and go to the Builder Panel on the right.
  5. Click + Add Input Data and select the required dimensions and measures (e.g., Version, Plants, Product, M1).

Step 3: Add and Execute the R Script

Click Add Script, and you will see four options: Editor, Console, Environment, and Preview.

Paste the following R script into the Editor section. (Note: Adjust dimension/measure names as per your model)

library(data.table)
library(plotly)

as.sunburstDF <- function(DF, valueCol = NULL){
  require(data.table)
  colNamesDF <- names(DF)
  
  if(is.data.table(DF)){
    DT <- copy(DF)
  } else {
    DT <- data.table(DF, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  }
  
  DT[, root := names(DF)[1]]
  colNamesDT <- names(DT)
  
  if(is.null(valueCol)){
    setcolorder(DT, c("root", colNamesDF))
  } else {
    setnames(DT, valueCol, "values", skip_absent=TRUE)
    setcolorder(DT, c("root", setdiff(colNamesDF, valueCol), "values"))
  }

  hierarchyCols <- setdiff(colNamesDT, "values")
  hierarchyList <- list()

  for(i in seq_along(hierarchyCols)){
    currentCols <- colNamesDT[1:i]
    if(is.null(valueCol)){
      currentDT <- unique(DT[, ..currentCols][, values := .N, by = currentCols], by = currentCols)
    } else {
      currentDT <- DT[, lapply(.SD, sum, na.rm = TRUE), by=currentCols, .SDcols = "values"]
    }
    setnames(currentDT, length(currentCols), "labels")
    hierarchyList[[i]] <- currentDT
  }

  hierarchyDT <- rbindlist(hierarchyList, use.names = TRUE, fill = TRUE)
  parentCols <- setdiff(names(hierarchyDT), c("labels", "values", valueCol))

  hierarchyDT[, parents := apply(.SD, 1, function(x){
    fifelse(all(is.na(x)), yes = NA_character_, no = paste(x[!is.na(x)], sep = ":", collapse = " - "))
  }), .SDcols = parentCols]

  hierarchyDT[, ids := apply(.SD, 1, function(x){
    paste(x[!is.na(x)], collapse = " - ")
  }), .SDcols = c("parents", "labels")]

  hierarchyDT[, c(parentCols) := NULL]
  return(hierarchyDT)
}

# Example data setup
data11 = Test_Model
data22 = subset(data11, select = c("Version", "Plants", "Product", "M1"))
colnames(data22) <- c("Version", "Plants", "Product", "M1")

DF <- as.data.table(data22)
setcolorder(DF, c("Version", "Plants", "Product", "M1"))
sunburstDF <- as.sunburstDF(DF, valueCol = "M1")

plot_ly(
  data = sunburstDF,
  ids = ~ids,
  labels = ~labels,
  parents = ~parents,
  values = ~values,
  type = 'sunburst',
  branchvalues = 'total',
  marker = list(
    colors = c('white', "e6ccb2", "79b2d2", "a1c65d", "fac723", "f29222", "e95e50", "ff92b1")
  )
)

Click Execute, and the preview pane will display the generated Sunburst chart. Hit Apply to render it on the story canvas.

Conclusion

Using R and Plotly inside SAP Analytics Cloud, you can build highly interactive and insightful Sunburst charts to visualize hierarchical datasets. This approach not only expands SAC's native visualization capabilities but also gives you flexibility to tailor visuals to your data story.

Explore more possibilities with R integration in SAC and turn your dashboards into truly dynamic visual experiences.

entry.title: Understanding incorrect Message Mapping and why Context matters
entry.content:
What are Contexts in Message Mapping?

Contexts allow for a logical grouping of data elements, influencing how data is processed during mapping. Each context can hold specific information that helps in deciding how data should be transformed.

 

 

Why Contexts are Important

 

  • Data Organization: Contexts help organize data, making it easier to see relationships and hierarchies.
  • Flexibility: They allow different data sets to be handled differently based on situations.
  • Performance: Using contexts can make data processing more efficient by simplifying the transformation steps.

 

 

Understanding Queues in Message Mapping

Queues temporarily hold messages as they move between processing stages, ensuring orderly data handling. They help manage messages between systems, especially when different systems process data at different speeds.

 

  • Purpose of Queues: Queues help buffer messages and synchronize data processing.

  • Integration with Contexts: Contexts help organize how messages are processed once in a queue and may trigger specific processing rules based on the data.

 

 

Managing Contexts

 

  • Defining Contexts: Clearly define contexts to represent different data areas when creating message mappings.
  • Using Context Functions: SAP offers tools to help change data within contexts, such as 'Context Switch' functions.
  • Context Variables: Use temporary context variables to retain values during processing, avoiding repeated calculations.
  • Conditional Logic: Use if-else statements to adjust the context based on incoming data from queues.
  • Nesting Contexts: Nest contexts for complex data structures, allowing better data management.

 

 

Hands-on example

 

With the basics out of the way, lets look at the information in a real world example, a problem that i had to resolve a while back. In one flow an issue arises, somehow data gets mixed up after message mapping. Users (Employees) have mostly correct information, but at some point Users have a correct ID, correct job description, etc. However, their first and last names are incorrect. How can this happen? But not only that, looking at the bottom of our Employees, the last few entries of our mapping results are basically empty.

 

 

Identifying an issue with source data

 

After a lot of searching, debugging, checking we find out that our mapping tries to look up some nodes that do not exist:

 

 

In our case, the "empInfo" tag is empty, this holds information about our employees, like the aforementioned firstName and lastName.

Debugging

 

So this mapping here is being ignored, as the nodes leading up to it do not exist and the whole mapping is skipped.This is where the queue comes in, but in a way that messes up our data. Let's have a look at what i am talking about:

 

 

To check out what exactly is happening with our data, it is necessary to provide a test input file. This can be done in a few different ways, like when trying to simulate mapping some examples which requires data given with a test file. Once we provide a file, we can check out what the input queue looks like and what it would look like after mapping:

 

The input node can display the input queue, which in our test case right now would look something like this:

 

 

Well that's fine but what are we looking at here? First entry, the <null> can simply be ignored, this is the start of the queue. Every white background entry means a value that will end up in our mapping result, which we can check here:

 


Every grey entry means a context change. Basically a new entry, in our case a new Employee. But right there, we can see two grey rows with "Brandon" in them. A white row is missing. That is the reason why our resulting mapped list of Employees firstName is messed up. Say we have 10 employee's first names and we want the result to also show these 10 names, with our given example we would end up with only 9 names. Because the context changed and no value was given.

 

 

Why fix the message mapping?

 

Why would an employee have no first name? Simple actually. Admin users, service users. If we were to exclude them from the mapping, filter them out before mapping, we would be done and could continue without a problem. Yes, that is a possibility. But then again, what other issues might arise with our given data? How much more would we have to filter out, would we even be aware of incorrect data? In our case alone, we found two more instances of the source data not being what we expected it to be. No, filtering is not the solution here.

 

Tools

 

What we need to correctly map everything thrown at us is to handle the context and handle the queue. Message mapping gives us a few tools that we can use.



This method allows us to handle every context change by ourselves. In the Advanced section there are several options, so we can change the context only when the value in the queue changes, only on empty values, etc.

 

 

This method can give us a default value if the input is empty.
With these two we changed the mapping on all of our input fields, so they all look like this now:

 

 

So we force our given queue to always give us something into our output and the context is forced to always change.
But still, we have issues. Because even if we make sure to always handle the context correctly, this does not matter if we can't even reach the input node during mapping. As we've seen earlier, the whole chain of nodes leading down to the firstName doesn't exist. So we created a perfectly fine mapping which is simply ignored by our message mapping.

 

The solution

 

The solution is simple but very easy to miss. When we select the input property and click the three-dots-menu, we are given a few selections.One of these options is the Context entry:

 


Here we can see our chain of nodes. In this case PerPersonal cannot be reached as many of its parent nodes are missing. So all need to do is select the lowest node that always exists, the node that we are sure is always present. In our case this was empInfo:

 

 

The result

 

With this new start of the context selected, and all of our additional methods for context manipulation, our output queue now looks like this:

 

 

The queue changed, so even if we have some missing nodes, our output would still have at least something, in this case an empty String (as provided in the default value mapping).
After we changed the context and added the node methods on all of our input fields, it was done. We mapped all of our data correctly, nothing was missing.
 
Success.

entry.title: HCM Brasil: Crédito do Trabalhador - eConsignado - Atualizações
entry.content:
Olá pessoal, 

Esse blog post tem por objetivo comunicar as principais atualizações sobre o Crédito do Trabalhador.

Em resposta à promulgação da medida provisória 1.292/2025 pelo governo no dia 12/03/2025, realizamos a primeira entrega do Crédito do Trabalhador no dia 05 de Maio de 2025.

As seguintes notas foram entregues para implementar as funcionalidades necessárias:

  • 3590652 - Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3590105 and SAP Note 3582460
  • 3582460 - Infotype 3453 - Payroll Installment Discount
  • 3590105 - HR Renewal - Infotype 3453 - Payroll Installment Discount
  • 3584013 - Payroll installment discount - new wage types
  • 3583606 - BRPID - Payroll installment discount - new payroll function
  • 3597079 - eSocial - Payroll Events - Data Extraction for eConsignado

Até o momento, também entregamos as seguintes notas de correção:

  • 3606635 - BRPID - Installment wage type not frozen during retroactive calculation
  • 3607774 - Infotype 3453 - Future record deletion correction
  • 3607908 - BRPID - Wage type MCP0 is incorrect generated when vacation starts in the next period
  • 3611106 - PRPRI - Incorrect calculation of loan installment wage type with family allowance benefit (salário família)

Cetifique-se que todas as notas estejam implementadas em seu sistema para que a solução esteja em conformidade com as mudanças legais.

Perguntas frequentes:

1. Por que as diferenças de 13º na Rescisão não estão sendo consideradas na composição da remuneração disponível?
Até a data de publicação do manual de orientação do empregador (em 16/05/2025), a única informação oficial sobre o 13º salário era que seus valores não deveriam ser considerados na composição da remuneração disponível do trabalhador.

Com base nessa diretriz, a SAP desenvolveu uma solução que não considera os valores de 13º nos cálculos de composição da remuneração disponível.

No entanto, após a publicação do manual, verificamos que essa regra continua válida, com uma exceção importante:

Os valores de 13º proporcional e 13º indenizado pagos na rescisão devem ser incluídos na remuneração disponível das rescisões.

Diante disso, já estamos ajustando a solução para considerar esses valores (proporcional e indenizado) na remuneração disponível especificamente em casos de rescisão.

Sabemos que essa adaptação é complexa, e por isso precisamos de um tempo para desenvolver, testar e entregar a solução completa.

Enquanto isso, existe uma possível solução de contorno:

  • Você pode customizar as rubricas /335 - 13º salário na rescisão e /336 - 13º salário indenização na view V_T51P6.

⚠️ IMPORTANTE:


Essa solução alternativa não resolve o problema por completo. Não é possivel realizar a dedução do INSS e IRRF sobre as diferenças do 13º, mesmo que as rubricas /303 - Trib.INSS (13º) e /403 - Tributo IRRF (13º) estejam parametrizadas na view V_T51P6.
Ou seja, a remuneração disponível na rescisão poderá ficar maior do que o valor correto.

Por isso, cabe a cada empresa avaliar se deseja ou não utilizar essa solução provisória até que a correção definitiva seja disponibilizada pela SAP.

2. Por que rubricas configuradas na tabela T51P6 não estão sendo subtraídas no cálculo de Remuneração Disponível (rubrica MCTN)?

As duas principais razões para uma rubrica não ser subtraída no cálculo da Remuneração Disponível são:

  1. A rubrica não está com sinal negativo na RT, o sinal da rubrica é fundamental para realização dos descontos.
  2. A rubrica ainda não está calculada/atualizada (disponível na RT) quando a função PRPRI é chamada no esquema de folha.

3. Por que a provisão de crédito do trabalhador nas férias não está sendo gerada quando há um split de férias em dois períodos?
Em casos de split de férias em dois períodos, o provisionamento para ambos os meses só será executado se houver entradas criadas para os dois períodos no IT3453.

Ressaltamos que este é um dos poucos cenários em que a regra de carregamento de dados mensal não se aplica.

Por exemplo, para um período de férias de 23 de Junho a 2 de Julho, o cliente deve criar duas entradas no IT3453 — uma com a data de lançamento para Junho e outra para Julho — garantindo a geração de provisões para ambos os meses.

Você pode acompanhar novas correções e atualizações nos seguintes canais:

  • Brasil Workzone
  • KBA 3588328 - eConsignado - Crédito do Trabalhador Guide
  • Regulatory Change Manager

Gostou desse post? Dê um Like e compartilhe o conteúdo com seus colegas.

Fiquem à vontade para deixar um feedback, comentário ou pergunta sobre este Blog post no espaço abaixo. E não esqueçam de seguir a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para ficarem ligados nas últimas notícias.

Atenciosamente,
Nathalia

entry.title: Previous year's total cannot be displayed in SAC reports
entry.content:
Hi Team，

Create a report that displays cumulative amounts in the SAC report.

Example:
In case of single month display April 2025 100 May 2025 100
In this cumulative display, April 2025 100 May 2025 200
I was able to display the measure for the current year without any problem,
The cumulative total for the previous year could not be produced.

Even with the attached story side limit measure.
It didn't seem to be possible to shift the previous value either.
Are there any other alternatives?

entry.title: ‌🇫🇷‌ French SAP User Group Meeting on HCM
entry.content:
We are pleased to invite you to an event focused on the latest developments in the SAP HCM Payroll for France (SAP HCM-PY-FR) module. This session will highlight the most recent and upcoming enhancements delivered by SAP, along with a detailed overview of how recent legal changes published in France are impacting SAP solutions.

This event is organised by USF French SAP User Group on HCM.

Agenda

  • Fait générateur : use contribution rates and thresholds of the for-period for active employees : Information on the latest decisions from the Institutions for a productive transition in 2027
  • New Procedures for non-maintenance garnishment starting 1 july 2025
  • End of exemption for non-prescribed periods in calculation of daily social security benefits (IJSS)
  • Technical Balance Contribution (CET) for Part-Time Employees Contributing Full-Time
  • PLF PLFSS 2025 : Exemption from employee contributions and Tax on overtime for Apprentice
  • DSN - Initiation Report of Variable Data (SADV) / update                              
  • DSN: New process to manage Fraction in DSN (i.e. in case of Judicial Receivership)
    - Manage retroaccounting on another fraction and fraction change during a month & DSN
    - Manage the creation of two DSN in the same month for the same SIRET in case of Judicial Receivership
  • Data Reliability in DSN:
    * Declare activity volumes (S21.G00.53)
    * FC 2713/2007: Field "Unit of measure" 53.003 – Value 40 Calendar days of the employment period considered in payroll calculation
    * Social Security ceiling in case of payment of a variable element during suspension of the employment contract

Speakers:
Catherine Lefrancois, Local Product Manager, SAP
Lionel Racca, Local Product Manager, SAP

Registration Link

entry.title: Coming Soon: Release Navigator for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Reducing the Intimidation of ERP Releases

It is widely acknowledged that software releases can be intimidating and overwhelming, particularly for those who lack adequate preparation. In times when releases seem daunting, the initial effort may feel more like a struggle to keep up. Other times, it often appears more convenient to inform your team, “We can simply wait for the next release and plan more effectively.” This scenario is commonplace, leading to a reactive approach to releases rather than a proactive one. Being reactive can create the sensation of merely striving to keep one’s “head above water” in an effort to maintain operational technology. To confront this issue, SAP Enterprise Support is unveiling a new tool designed to streamline access to the latest release content curated by SAP experts. This tool assembles and organizes information into a user-friendly digital library.

Introducing the Release Navigator: a resource aimed at helping customers derive maximum business value from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition by centralizing release information and content.  We encourage you to explore this valuable resource to enhance your approach to managing technology upgrades. 

The Release Navigator will be available later this month on the Release Ready page which you can access here:

 

 

 

Saving Time & Effort

The Release Navigator helps users and administrators understand new features that are coming with the release. It also assists with:

  1. Guidance on Releases: It offers guidance on important dates and new functionalities are included in the latest release, helping businesses to better prepare for updates.
  2. Support Materials: Provides access to various support materials such as release blogs, product documentation, guided demos, and videos that assist in leveraging new features effectively (refer to Figure 1: Product Specific page).
  3. Change Management: Helps with change management by ensuring that all stakeholders are informed about new features and can plan training or process adjustments accordingly.

Figure 1: Product Specific page

 

At launch, the following topics will be organized by the following:

Lines of Business (LoB)
  • Asset Management
  • Finance
  • Human Resources
  • Manufacturing
  • R&D/Engineering
  • Sales
  • Service
  • Sourcing & Procurement
  • Supply Chain
Industries
  • Automotive
  • Banking
  • Defense & Security
  • Insurance
  • Oil & Gas
  • Public Sector
  • Retail & Fashion
  • Utilities
Enterprise Technology
  • SAP Fiori
Artificial Intelligence (AI)
  • SAP Joule

Additional release content and information pertaining to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition that is not specific to a particular line of business (LoB), industry, enterprise technology, or artificial intelligence can be found on the General page (refer to Figure 2: General page). This section also provides links to other Release Navigators related to various SAP products and services, including Business Technology Platform and Supply Chain Management.

Figure 2: General page

 

Innovation Plan

To minimize disruptions to your user experience, the Release Navigator for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition incorporates new UX elements once a year, during the October update cycle, aligning with the introduction of new release content. The updates to the Release Navigator are fully automated, requiring no manual intervention.

 
For comprehensive details on product release schedules, please consult the Release Calendar card. This resource includes plans for UX innovation and content updates through 2025.
 
Find more details below:
 

 

Connect with Us

We value your input and encourage you to share any feedback or specific content requests for future updates to the Release Navigator. Please feel free to complete our 2-question anonymous survey or contribute your suggestions in the Comments section below this post.

You can also leave your feedback directly in the Release Navigator by selecting the User Feedback inside the side menu (refer to Figure 3: User Feedback page).

 

Figure 3: User Feedback page

Best Run,
Andrew Ford

entry.title: Mapping Salary from REC Offer Template to EC Compensation Info
entry.content:
Hi everyone, 

Our customer requires to map the Salary from Offer Template to the Compensation Information Pay Components. 

Field in Offer Template: Salary (general string)

Pay Components in Comp Info: BI-weekly or Hourly 

There are two salary types:

  • Bi-weekly - the annualized amount is entered in the Offer Template in the field "Salary" (string)
  • Hourly - the hourly amount will be entered in the Offer Template in the field "Salary" (string)

The idea is to map them in a way that one of these two values, depending on the Employee Subgroup, is mapped to either the Pay Component or Pay Component Group. 

Does anyone know if this is possible to do via Recruit to Hire template or any custom solution?

Thank you in advance. 

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central - Employee Profile  SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting

Communication users usually don't have roles assigned.

entry.title: Configuration of Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) on SAP BTP for SAP Datasphere Tenants
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere is increasingly adopted in multi-tier landscapes (Dev, QA, Prod), where managing the lifecycle of analytics content in a controlled and traceable manner becomes essential. SAP Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) addresses this need by enabling secure, seamless, and centralized transport of artifacts across SAP Datasphere tenants through SAP BTP.

CTMS is now the recommended approach moving forward while transporting business-critical data from one Datasphere Tenant to another, as it provides complete data governance and logs while transporting objects. 

In this blog, we’ll guide you step-by-step through the configuration and setup of CTMS for Datasphere.




Prerequisites

  • BTP Global Account Admin Access

  • SAP Datasphere DW Admin Access

  • CTMS enabled on the BTP Global Account

Step 1: Enable CTMS in Your BTP Subaccount

  1. Go to SAP BTP Cockpit → Global Account → Entity Assignments.

  2. Select the subaccount for CTMS. (Usually your Dev Datasphere subaccount, You need to configure CTMS only on one subaccount where your Datasphere source account is.)

  3. Click Edit → Add Service Plan

     

Step 2: Subscribe to CTMS and Create an Instance

  1. Add a Service Plan → Cloud Transport Management → Choose the Appropriate Plans


     

  2. In the subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.

  3. Search for cloud transport management, and create a subscription.

     

Download the generated JSON key after creation.

 

 

Please Note: If the key is not available/not visible, Go inside the CTMS configuration menu --> Create a new service key --> Just give a name to the key and hit create.  

 

 

Step 3: Connect CTMS with SAP Datasphere

  1. Log in to your SAP Datasphere tenant.

  2. Navigate to Transport → Export.

  3. Select the My Content folder.

  4. Click on Settings.

  5. Choose Add New System.

  6. Upload the JSON key file you downloaded during the CTMS instance creation in BTP.


Step 4a: Create OAuth Client in SAP Datasphere

  1. Log in to your SAP Datasphere tenant.

  2. Go to System → Administrator → App Integration.

  3. Click on Add OAuth Client.

  4. Fill in the following:

    • Purpose: API Access

    • Access: Analytics Content Network Interaction

    • Authorization Grant Type: Client Credentials

  5. Save the Client ID and Client Secret.
    🔒Note: The client secret is shown only once. Store it securely for later use.

Step 4b: Map SAP Datasphere Tenant in BTP (Create Destination)

  1. Go to your SAP BTP Cockpit.

  2. Navigate to Connectivity → Destinations → Create Destination.

  3. Enter the following destination details:
  • URL: (Format) https:// <DSP Tenant>/api/v1/content/deploy/
  • Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
  • Client ID: You can find this ID from the OAuth Client you created in the previous step. (In Datasphere)


  • Client Secret: When you create an OAuth Client in Datasphere, you get an option to view the client key. Note: Client secret is only visible once while creating an OAuth Client, make sure you take note of it. Otherwise, you need to create a new OAuth client to get a new key.
  • Token Service URL:(Format) <Token URL> ?grant_type=client_credentials. 
    The token URL can be found in the Datasphere -> App Integration section.
  • Repeat this step for each SAP Datasphere tenant (Dev, QA, Prod) in your landscape.

    Important Note: Customer needs to add the destination of each datasphere tenant they have. For Example, if the customer is using a 3-tier DSP landscape, and they want a transport layer like (Dev -> QA -> Prod) in these cases we need to add a destination for each DSP tenant

Step 5: Logging into the CTMS Portal:

When you logging into Ctms for the 1st time, it says a pop-up saying you don't have authorization. You need to log out from ctms manually and log in again. If It still says Authorization missing, please refer to the security section of the blog. 

Step 6: Define CTMS Landscape and Routes

  1. Open CTMS Cockpit → Landscape Wizard.

  2. Select a landscape template (e.g., Three-Node Landscape).

  3. For each node:

    • Name: SAP recommends having a proper naming convention in order to understand nodes travelling from one to another. 

    • Allow Upload: Only on the Dev/source node.

    • Forward Mode: Auto or Manual. 

      • Auto: This would allow you to forward the uploaded package to the next Node automatically.
      • Manual: Users need to trigger the forwarding of uploaded packages to the next Node.

    • Content-Type: Leave blank for Dev (if it doesn't allow the blank value, you can use Application Content); use Application Content for QA and Prod.

    • Destination: Choose the appropriate BTP destination. Please Note: The destination here would be from CTMS to your Datasphere tenant. 

Note: Route 1 shows the data will travel from Node 1(Dev)  --> Node 2 (QA) and Route 2 shows data will travel from Node 2 (QA) --> Node 3 (Prod)

 

Step 7: Transporting Packages

  1. In Datasphere Dev tenant, go to Transport → Export → select a package.

  2. Choose Upload to SAP Cloud Transport Management node.

  3. Select Node 2 (QA).

  4. Click Upload.

  5. In the CTMS portal, You can go to Transport Request and click on the Import All tab to import the package manually. 

    Note: CTMS will directly transport the objects to the target tenant. For example, if you have a local table 'sales' inside a space called 'finance', in the destination datasphere tenant, once the transport is succeeded, you will see the sales table in the available directly in finance space in the target system. 

 
Authorization:

Roles required for getting full access to CTMS features:

  • Administrator
  • Landscape Operator
  • Transport Operator
  • Import Operator
  • Export Operator

In addition to the above roles, if a user wants to have the rights to delete, import transport automatically, disable, or remove; the user needs to create an additional role in SAP BTP: 

  1. Go to BTP -> Roles -> Transport Operator -> Create Role

     

  2. Give the Role Name -> Source Static -> Enter the Node Value.
    Note: This node value should be the same which we created in Step 6 (Transport Landscape Wizard). Click on Enter after every node is entered. 


  3. In the Next Step -> Select 'Transport Operator'  in the Role Collection.
  4. Make sure Transport Operator TMS is added to your administrator role collection as well.

Conclusion: This guide sets up a complete CTMS workflow for SAP Datasphere across a typical Dev → QA → Prod landscape using SAP BTP. You can now securely and efficiently transport your content, manage lifecycle, and reduce manual errors.

SAP Datasphere SAP Cloud Transport Management

entry.title: Pin/Job not appearing in maps component for CSB
entry.content:
Hi all, 

We are currently running into an issue where some job reqs are not appearing on the maps component. There are a couple of jobs that aren't appearing with the pin typically seen on the left side of the number. Without the pin, we're unable to click and see the popup containing the list of jobs in the area. UDM is enabled for this environment

I've completed the following checks:

-I reviewed the location object and can confirm that the longitude/latitude values for the location are accurate.

-The location field on the job req is using the correct field type=object.

-The mapping from RCM to RMK is correct 

-The daily indexing job in provisioning running successfully.

-The job is posted and not expired

 I noticed that when I searched for the job req number on the component, the pin suddenly appeared and the job was selectable, however, when the page is refreshed, the job pin disappears again. Has anyone run into this?

entry.title: Re: Template Allocation and Splitting Structure
entry.content:
Hi santhoshabh,

It is fine to use template allocation for actual rate calculation. The problem you are facing is probably based on the splitting accounts used in the splitting structure.

Try to review your expected results, there may be some overlapping GL account that is used in association with more than one activity type. If that is the case it is how it normally works.

If the same GL account in the same cost center has multiple activity type you need to use alternative methods (i.e. equivalence number; using multiple Cost center; secondary cost elements).

Best regards

entry.title: Re: list compatible RF scanners for WM in SAP S4hana in p...
entry.content:
Hello,

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 2502 only supports RF scanner integration via SAP Fiori-based mobile apps—not traditional SAP ITS Mobile or custom RF transactions.

To ensure compatibility, your Zebra TC8000, M33, and OLN420 devices must meet the following criteria:

  • Support modern web browsers (e.g., Chrome, Edge) or Android OS with Fiori compatibility
  • Have access to SAP Launchpad via browser or a managed mobile environment (MDM)

How to Check:

  • Test access to SAP Fiori Launchpad from each scanner using its browser.
  • Validate usability of Warehouse Management apps (e.g., “Run Outbound Process,” “Confirm Warehouse Tasks”) via touch input.
  • Confirm network/firewall access to your S/4HANA Cloud tenant.

For full certification, consult your MDM provider and Zebra documentation, or contact SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) for custom UI scenarios.

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: Can't install ADT in Eclipse
entry.content:
Here are two steps to solve that problem:
Make sure that eclipse.ini already has the following two options after-vmargs:

-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStore=NONE
-Djavax.net.ssl.trustStoreType=Windows-ROOT

The second step is to import the certificate from the website https://tools.eu1.hana.ondemand.com/ into your Windows.

entry.title: Recommendations added to the Case Overview Report in Customer Insights Dashboard
entry.content:
We're excited to announce a significant enhancement to the automated Case Overview Report (COR) available in the Customer Insights Dashboard (CID) through the "PDF Report Generator" menu.

This upgraded feature now includes additional slides that provide customized recommendations, enhancing the detailed analysis the report already offers.

The new recommendation slides are crafted based on an in-depth comparison of overall average calculations against specific customer behaviors. This advanced analytical approach allows the report to offer actionable insights that are directly relevant to your unique operational context. The recommendations cover a comprehensive range of case-related KPIs such as:

  • Number of Priority 1 cases,
  • Channels used,
  • Processing times,
  • Error categories,

and more.

 

By integrating these automated recommendations into your reporting, you gain a powerful tool that not only highlights areas of concern but also suggests operational adjustments and improvements. This can significantly enhance decision-making processes, allowing you to optimize strategies and operations effectively based on solid, data-driven insights.

Utilize the enhanced Case Overview Report in CID and take advantage of the new automated recommendations feature. It's designed to boost your operational efficiency and provide you with the tools to achieve greater success in your business endeavours.

Stay updated by subscribing to this blog and visiting the SAP Support Portal / Customer Insights.

SAP for Me is made for you!

Related blogs: Automated Case Overview Report  or all related blogs to ‘Customer Insights’ reporting dashboard.

entry.title: Partner Benefits Catalog for SAP Partners
entry.content:
SAP’s partner ecosystem is built on a shared mission—to deliver customer success together. And to help our partners maximize the value of their collaboration with SAP, we’ve enhanced the SAP Partner Benefits Catalog, making it even easier to identify and access the services that matter most to your business.

What’s New? Personalized, Tier-Based Benefits

The SAP Partner Benefits Catalog is a central resource that showcases a broad range of benefits and fee based services available to partners across all strategic solutions and the customer value journey. With the latest enhancements, partners can now:

  • Filter benefits based on their PartnerEdge level (Silver, Gold, or Platinum)

  • View services that are exclusive to their current tier

This new experience brings greater transparency and personalization, allowing partners to quickly identify high-value services—from go-to-market support and enablement offerings to co-innovation and demand generation programs.

Why It Matters

The enhanced catalog is more than just a list—it’s a strategic tool that helps partners:

✅ Plan their benefit utilization more effectively
✅ Unlock hidden value based on their current partnership level
✅ Strategically grow their business with SAP support

Whether you’re looking to scale faster, improve customer engagement, or expand your SAP solution knowledge, the catalog provides clear, actionable insights tailored to your partner journey.

Get Started Today

Log in to the Partner Benefits Catalog and explore services available based on your PartnerEdge level, designed to support your growth—every step of the way.

entry.title: Re: How to get quota values & its corresponding year and...
entry.content:
@kr_pavankumar 

Kindly adjust the below SQL to your business requirement to extract but below SQL statement will be providing an hint to connect various tables.

---- PLQU , Quota -------------------------
SELECT Q.NAME,
       Q.DESCRIPTION,
       POS.NAME,
       FV.VALUE
  FROM CS_QUOTA Q
       INNER JOIN CS_QUOTA_VARIABLES QV
          ON Q.QUOTASEQ = QV.QUOTASEQ
       INNER JOIN CS_PERIOD PER
          ON     PER.NAME = 'January 2019'
             AND QV.Effectivestartdate < Per.Enddate
             AND QV.Effectiveenddate >= Per.Enddate
       INNER JOIN CS_CALENDAR CAL
          ON     CAL.NAME = 'Main Monthly Calendar'
             AND CAL.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
             AND CAL.CALENDARSEQ = PER.CALENDARSEQ
       INNER JOIN CS_PERIODTYPE PT
          ON     PT.PERIODTYPESEQ = PER.PERIODTYPESEQ
             AND PT.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
             AND PT.NAME = 'month'
       INNER JOIN CS_VARIABLE VAR
          ON VAR.RULEELEMENTSEQ = QV.VARIABLESEQ
             AND (VAR.Effectivestartdate < Per.Enddate
                  AND VAR.Effectiveenddate >= Per.Enddate
                  OR     VAR.EFFECTIVEENDDATE > PER.STARTDATE
                     AND VAR.EFFECTIVEENDDATE <= PER.ENDDATE
                     AND VAR.ISLAST = 1)
       INNER JOIN Cs_Variableassignment Va
          ON     VAR.RULEELEMENTSEQ = Va.VARIABLESEQ
             AND QV.VARIABLESEQ = VA.VARIABLESEQ
             AND VA.Effectivestartdate < Per.Enddate
             AND VA.Effectiveenddate >= Per.Enddate
       INNER JOIN Cs_fixedvalue fv
          ON fv.Ruleelementseq = Va.Assignmentseq AND fv.modelseq = 0
             AND (FV.Effectivestartdate < Per.Enddate
                  AND FV.Effectiveenddate >= Per.Enddate
                  OR     FV.EFFECTIVEENDDATE > PER.STARTDATE
                     AND FV.EFFECTIVEENDDATE <= PER.ENDDATE
                     AND FV.ISLAST = 1)
             AND FV.PERIODTYPESEQ = PT.PERIODTYPESEQ
       INNER JOIN CS_POSITION POS
          ON Va.Ruleelementownerseq = Pos.Ruleelementownerseq
             AND (POS.Effectivestartdate < Per.Enddate
                  AND POS.Effectiveenddate >= Per.Enddate
                  OR     POS.EFFECTIVEENDDATE > PER.STARTDATE
                     AND POS.EFFECTIVEENDDATE <= PER.ENDDATE
                     AND POS.ISLAST = 1)
 WHERE     Q.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND QV.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND VAR.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND VA.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND FV.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND POS.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND PER.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE ('01/01/2200', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
       AND Q.NAME = 'QUOTANAME'
       AND POS.NAME = 'POSITIONNAMEHERE';

entry.title: Supplier Invoice approval workflow - Invoice currency

entry.title: Modifying RFPOSXEXT Structure Fields in FBL3N Using BTE
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are currently working on a requirement to enhance the FBL3N report by populating values for fields such as LIFNR and EBELN.

During our analysis and after reviewing several SAP Community blogs, we found that BTE 00001650 is a viable solution for this enhancement. However, we have encountered the following issues:

  1. The standard structure RFPOSXEXT already contains the fields U_LIFNR and U_EBELN. But upon debugging BTE 00001650, we observed that these fields are not present in the internal tables I_POSTAB and E_POSTAB.

  2. To overcome this, we appended the required fields to the structures RFPOS and RFPOSX and then executed the report RFPOSXEXTEND. As a result, the structure RFPOSXEXT was updated correctly, and we were able to populate LIFNR and EBELN in the BTE logic, which were displayed as additional columns in the FBL3N output.

  3. Our main requirement is to populate these values specifically for documents with type 'RE', where currently these fields appear blank.

  4. We would like to confirm whether it is technically possible to populate values in the existing standard fields U_LIFNR and U_EBELN instead of creating and using new custom fields for this purpose.

We would appreciate your guidance or suggestions on how best to handle this scenario.

entry.title: SAP Cloud ALM Health Monitoring Integration Suite BTP Integrations and APIs
entry.content:

Thanks


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Health Monitoring Integration Suite BTP...
entry.content:
The KBA 3426195 discusses how to identify the products supported by SAP Cloud ALM, as well as the metrics offered by the various use cases (e.g. Health Monitoring). 

Currently SAP Integration Suite (API Management) is only supported by the Health Monitoring use case. The Health Monitoring Content page outlines that only a single metric is currently available: Certificate Validity.

Currently SAP Cloud ALM for Operations does not offer a use case to monitor APIs directly. Integrations are typically monitored using the Integration & Exception Monitoring use case. However, this use case does not yet support SAP Integration Suite (API Management).

If you wish to influence the future development of SAP Cloud ALM and provide direction to our Developers in relation to this topic, please use the options outlined in this KBA:
3391676 - SAP Continuous Influence for SAP Cloud ALM

entry.title: Service call module update date and create time into UTC format of MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss query
entry.content:
Hello experts.

I am needing to get the service call module update date & time (table OSCL) into a single column in the format of 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss' and then have it converted to UTC. Ultimately, I will be using this query in a Boyum P&D Report Action. I have seen posts before (but cannot find now) where the SQL function STUFF() was used but I know that each of those examples were for marketing document tables. Please direct me to those posts if I am mistaken.

The OSCL table does not record time with seconds, therefore the max number in characters/numbers in the time fields will be 4 (i.e., 1344 for 13:44, instead of ORDR's 134423 for 13:44:23).

I am able to perform the query below in MS SQL as well as the SAP B1 query editor when the time field has at least 3 characters (e.g., on or after 1am) but the FORMAT function fails when it is less than 3.

Is possible to query for the update date & time (or the create date & time) into a single column without having to perform a case statement to evaluate the length of time field (which could be from 1 - 4 characters long [2 for 00:02 and 932 for 09:32 and 1748 for 17:48])? 

FORMAT(CONVERT(DATETIME, CONVERT(VARCHAR(10), OSCL.UpdateDate, 101)+' '+FORMAT(OSCL.UpdateTime, '##:##')) AT TIME ZONE 'Eastern Standard Time' AT TIME ZONE 'UTC', 'MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm:ss')

 Thank you in advance for any assistance you can provide.

entry.title: Re: Use Cases on SAP Cloud ALM itself
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM itself is given an entry in the Landscape Management app, because some of the Operations use cases have metrics that can be collected and reviewed in those apps. And like other products, those use cases and data collection can only be enabled from their specific applications (e.g. Health Monitoring). So you can follow the same steps outlined in KBA 3616000.

  1. Log onto SAP Cloud ALM.
  2. Select the relevant Operations app (e.g. Health Monitoring).
  3. Select the "scope" button on the top right of the page.
  4. Use the search pop-up to locate the "SAP Cloud ALM" service.
    • You may need to edit the filters so that all systems are displayed in the results, not just the ones already configured.
  5. Select the "SAP Cloud ALM" entry to add it to the scope and click "apply".
  6. Click the "Configuration" button.
  7. If the option is available in the use case, enable the data collection by setting the toggle to "ON".
  8. Click the "SAP Cloud ALM" text.
  9. Depending on the use case, enable data collection or configure filters for the categories supported for the selected system/service product type. 
  10. Data should start to appear in the operations app.

Please note, as per KBA 3616000, some use cases (e.g. Alert Notification) can only be enabled by enabling alerting or notifications (e-mail/chat) via an event in one of the operations use cases. The KBA has the full set of information on this, and other specific use cases.

entry.title: Re: Supplier Invoice approval workflow - Invoice currency
entry.content:
Hello @Gohar 

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the Supplier Invoice Approval Workflow only allows threshold conditions in the company code currency. Group currency is not supported, and customer-specific enhancements like BAdIs (e.g., SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL) are unavailable in the public cloud.

 Recommended Workaround:

Use in-app extensibility via the Custom Fields and Logic app:

  • Create a custom field (e.g. “Group Currency Approval Level”) on the supplier invoice.
  • Implement custom logic to derive its value based on the invoice amount in group currency.
  • Use this custom field in your workflow conditions instead of relying on the invoice amount field.

 SAP Help References:

  • Manage Workflows for Supplier Invoices – SAP Help

This approach respects the restrictions of public cloud while still allowing you to incorporate group currency thresholds logically.

Thank you and kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: How to use Product migration object for Retail
entry.content:
Dear experts,

It's advisable to always check the official documentation of any Migration Object you're handling, as it consistently has useful information about it. Considering your system version and release, the relevant documentation for your context would be the following one:

  • Product | SAP Help Portal

In it, you'll find that it leads to even further information about this Migration Object in regards to how to use it for retail purposes:

  • Product for SAP S/4HANA retail for merchandise management | SAP Help Portal

It'd be best if both documentations were fully reviewed by your team, so you can fully understand if the options provided by the Migration Object suits your business needs.

I hope this helps with your query!

Best regards,
SAP Support
